title: YAML frontmatter injection via unescaped backslash/newline in extract-knowledge field emission
description: quoteRelevancePath() escapes only the double-quote character (value.replace(/"/g, '\\"')) but NOT backslashes or newlines (packages/server/src/services/extract-knowledge.ts:718-720). Every caller-supplied string field written into the YAML frontmatter of a pending KB file flows through this function: summary (line 625), tags (631), relevance_paths (639), intent_clues (650), tech_stack (654), impact (658), must_read_if (662), evidence_paths (676). The corresponding zod schemas are plain z.string()/z.array(z.string()) with NO charset or newline restriction (packages/shared/src/schemas/api-contracts.ts:683-740). A value ending in a single backslash (e.g. summary='foo\\') renders as `summary: "foo\\"` — the trailing backslash escapes the closing quote, breaking YAML parsing and allowing a crafted value to inject arbitrary additional frontmatter keys (e.g. forging layer:, type:, maturity:, x-fabric-idempotency-key:, or a status: active to bypass review). The NEW-31 prompt-injection sanitizer (extract-knowledge.ts:46-75) only redacts instruction-style patterns; it does not neutralize backslashes or newlines, so the YAML-breaking primitive survives sanitization. Because fab_extract_knowledge is an MCP tool whose inputs are produced by an LLM/skill (a prompt-injection-reachable surface), a poisoned session transcript could steer the emitted frontmatter.
fix_direction: Replace the hand-rolled quoting with a real YAML scalar serializer, or at minimum escape backslashes before quotes (value.replace(/\\/g, '\\\\').replace(/"/g, '\\"')) AND reject/escape embedded newlines. Better: add a regex/length guard at the zod schema layer (api-contracts.ts) for single-line frontmatter fields and round-trip the assembled frontmatter through a YAML parser before write to fail closed.

title: git clone URL argument injection in `fabric install --global <url>`
description: mountStoreFromRemote() passes the user-supplied CLI <url> positionally to `git clone` without a `--` separator: execFileSync("git", ["clone", url, dest], ...) (packages/cli/src/install/run-global-install.ts:38, called from line 88 with options.url straight from the CLI arg). Because there is no `--` end-of-options marker before url, a value beginning with a dash (e.g. `--upload-pack=...` or other git transport options such as ext::) is parsed by git as an OPTION rather than a repository, which git's transport layer can turn into local command execution. execFileSync avoids shell injection, but not git's own argument/transport injection. Threat model is local — the user is supplying the URL to their own CLI — so impact is limited (self-inflicted), hence low; still a hardening gap given the URL may be copy-pasted from an untrusted source (e.g. a README badge or shared store link).
fix_direction: Insert a `--` separator before the URL: ["clone", "--", url, dest]. Additionally validate the URL scheme against an allowlist (https://, git@, ssh://) and reject leading '-' and the ext::/fd:: transports before invoking git.

context.notes: Discovered by security perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by inserting `--` before the clone URL in git argv; verified by run-global-install arg-safety tests.

title: Full project-tree walk + read+sha256 of every test file on EVERY recall/plan-context call
description: computeKnowledgeTestIndex (called unconditionally inside buildKnowledgeMeta) calls findFabricVerifyAnnotations -> findTestFiles, which performs a recursive readdir walk of the ENTIRE project tree (every dir except .git/.fabric/node_modules/dist/build/coverage) and then readFile + sha256 + line-split of EVERY *.test.ts/*.spec.ts file in the repo, scanning each line with a regex for `@fabric-verify` annotations. buildKnowledgeMeta has NO mtime/revision short-circuit, so this runs on every loadActiveMeta / loadActiveMetaOrStale, which are invoked on every fab_plan_context call (planContext line 214) AND every fab_get_knowledge_sections call (knowledge-sections.ts line 141). A single fab_recall therefore walks the whole source tree and reads+hashes every test file at least twice. On this repo (200+ source files, ~dozens of test files) that is a full-tree fs scan per MCP recall — the most expensive operation on the hottest path, gated by nothing.
fix_direction: Cache the knowledge-test index keyed by a cheap stat-based fingerprint (max mtime of knowledge/ + test files, or a dir-listing hash) and only re-walk when it changes; or decouple computeKnowledgeTestIndex from the read-path buildKnowledgeMeta entirely (the test-annotation index is only needed by doctor, not by recall). At minimum, gate findTestFiles behind the same mtime check used to decide whether agents.meta.json actually drifted.

context.notes: Discovered by performance perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

title: buildKnowledgeMeta re-reads + re-hashes every knowledge file on every read-path call (no cache)
description: loadActiveMeta / loadActiveMetaOrStale always call buildKnowledgeMeta(projectRoot) purely to compare the derived revision against the on-disk revision. buildKnowledgeMeta -> computeKnowledgeBasedAgentsMeta does findKnowledgeFiles (readdir over decisions/pitfalls/guidelines/models/processes/pending in BOTH team and personal roots) then readFile + sha256 + frontmatter-parse of EVERY knowledge .md (knowledge-meta-builder.ts:193-218), plus stableStringify (flattenKeys+sort over the whole meta, line 302-303). The contextCache 'meta' slot only caches readAgentsMeta's parsed JSON — it does NOT cache buildKnowledgeMeta. So every fab_plan_context and every fab_get_knowledge_sections re-scans and re-hashes the entire KB corpus from disk. fab_recall does this twice per call (planContext + getKnowledgeSections). Cost scales O(knowledge files x file size) per recall with zero memoization. [scalability note] The contextCache meta TTL is only 5s (plan-context.ts:507), so under steady recall traffic this full-KB re-read/re-hash effectively runs per request; cost scales linearly with KB entry count.
fix_direction: Memoize buildKnowledgeMeta output keyed by a directory-fingerprint (file count + max mtime over the knowledge subdirs), reusing the existing contextCache infrastructure / file_watch invalidation. The revision-drift comparison can short-circuit on the fingerprint without reading file bodies when nothing changed.

title: Full events.jsonl read + Zod-validate of every line on every recall (3x per fab_recall), only to surface rare redirects
description: readEventLedger reads the entire events.jsonl, splits all lines, JSON.parses and runs eventLedgerEventSchema.safeParse on EVERY line, builds the full events array, THEN applies 4 chained .filter() passes (event-ledger.ts:262-283). The event_type filter is applied post-parse, so even loadIdRedirectMap (which only wants knowledge_id_redirect events) pays to parse+validate the entire ledger. loadIdRedirectMap is called in planContext (plan-context.ts:359) AND again in recall (recall.ts:92) AND again in getKnowledgeSections (knowledge-sections.ts:114) — so a single fab_recall does 3 full-ledger scans+validations. The file is explicitly allowed to grow to 50MB before a warning fires (event-ledger.ts:28,200), making this an unbounded per-request cost. [MERGED scalability dup] Same root: readEventLedger reads the whole file into a string and Zod-parses every line BEFORE applying the type filter (no pushdown/streaming; event-ledger.ts:222), and id-redirect loadIdRedirectMap (id-redirect.ts:41) invokes this on every plan-context AND recall call — ~3x per fab_recall. Growth angle: at the documented 50MB ledger this is a full read + hundreds-of-K-line parse on the hot path purely to surface rare redirect events.
fix_direction: Add an early per-line event_type prefilter (cheap substring/JSON key check) before Zod safeParse, or maintain a small in-memory cache of the redirect map invalidated on append. De-duplicate the redundant loadIdRedirectMap calls across planContext/recall/getKnowledgeSections within one recall (compute once, thread through). Replace the 4 chained .filter() with a single pass.

title: BM25/locality score recomputed for every item on every comparator invocation (score-in-sort antipattern)
description: sortDescriptionItems builds the BM25 model once, but then sorts via compareDescriptionIndexItems which calls scoreDescriptionItem for BOTH the left and right item on EVERY pairwise comparison (plan-context.ts:703-716). Array.sort makes O(n log n) comparisons, so each candidate's score is recomputed ~log n times instead of once. scoreDescriptionItem is not trivial: per call it runs bm25.scoreDoc (loops query terms), a vector-map lookup, Date.parse, and a nested loop over relevance_paths x targetPaths with localityTier doing regex .search + string splits (plan-context.ts:798-808). Net effective cost is roughly O(n log n x queryTerms) + O(n log n x relevancePaths x targetPaths) of redundant work per plan-context, all avoidable by scoring each item once.
fix_direction: Precompute each item's score once into a parallel array (or Schwartzian transform: map to {item, score} then sort on the cached score), so the comparator only subtracts two numbers. Eliminates the log n score-recompute factor and the repeated locality nested-loop.

context.notes: Discovered by performance perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by precomputing plan-context scores before sort; verified by plan-context tests.

resolution: Resolved by precomputing plan-context scores before sort; verified by plan-context tests.

title: documentTextForItem + tokenize run twice per candidate during ranking
description: On the query path each candidate's selection text is built and tokenized redundantly: buildBm25Model tokenizes documentTextForItem(item) for every item (plan-context.ts:552-554, tokenize is a regex-loop CJK bigram splitter in text-tokenize.ts), and separately buildVectorScores is fed documentTextForItem(item) for every item (plan-context.ts:315). documentTextForItem itself rebuilds a joined string (spread of summary/intent_clues/tech_stack/impact/entities/tags) each time it is called. With score-in-sort (separate finding) the locality path also re-derives dirname/package via regex repeatedly. The per-candidate text construction + tokenization is duplicated rather than computed once and reused across BM25 + vector.
fix_direction: Build documentTextForItem(item) once per candidate into a map, and tokenize once; pass the cached token arrays to buildBm25Model and the cached text strings to buildVectorScores. Avoids double string-build and double tokenize per candidate.

title: O(selectedIds x nodes) linear node scan in getKnowledgeSections body fetch
description: getKnowledgeSections resolves bodies via selectedStableIds.map(id => findRuleNode(meta, id)) (knowledge-sections.ts:143). findRuleNode does a full Object.entries(meta.nodes) linear scan for each id (knowledge-sections.ts:298-324). In the common rc.37 fab_recall path the AI 'picks all' candidates, so selectedStableIds ~= every node, giving O(n^2) over the node set per recall. A single stable_id -> node Map built once would make this O(n).
fix_direction: Build a Map<stableId, node> from meta.nodes once at the top of getKnowledgeSections (and reuse it for both findRuleNode lookups and validation), turning the per-id O(nodes) scan into O(1).

title: O(metaEntries x ruleFiles) removal check using Array.includes in knowledge-sync
description: Both ensureKnowledgeFresh and reconcileKnowledge detect removed entries by iterating metaEntries and calling ruleFiles.includes(relPath) (knowledge-sync.ts:496 and :620). ruleFiles is a plain string[], so .includes is O(ruleFiles); nested inside the loop over metaEntries this is O(metaEntries x ruleFiles). ensureKnowledgeFresh runs on hot-path MCP consumers (the cooldown gate mitigates it to ~once/500ms, but it still fires whenever drift is suspected) and reconcileKnowledge runs on every auto-heal. Cheap to make O(n) with a Set.
fix_direction: Construct `const ruleFileSet = new Set(ruleFiles)` once and use ruleFileSet.has(relPath) in both removal-detection loops.

title: Per-call relevanceTargetPaths nested loop in locality scoring (no early exit)
description: scoreDescriptionItem locality computation loops relevancePaths x targetPaths and calls localityTier for every pair, where localityTier runs up to two regex .search calls plus split/filter on both strings (plan-context.ts:798-822, 813-858). It always exhausts the full cartesian product even after finding the max tier (LOCALITY_SAME_FILE=100 can never be beaten yet the loop continues). Combined with the score-in-sort recompute this multiplies, but on its own it is a minor per-candidate inefficiency.
fix_direction: Break out of the loops once `best === LOCALITY_SAME_FILE`, and precompute dirname/packageRoot for each path once instead of re-deriving via regex inside the inner loop.

context.notes: Discovered by performance perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by early-exiting locality scoring once LOCALITY_SAME_FILE is reached.

title: PreToolUse hook appends edit-counter / events.jsonl / silence-counter with bare appendFileSync — no advisory lock (multi-window interleave)
description: The PreToolUse narrow hook fires on every Edit/Write/MultiEdit, making it the single highest-frequency, most-concurrency-exposed write surface in Fabric. Three functions append to SHARED, non-session-scoped files with plain appendFileSync and zero serialization: appendEditIntentToLedger -> .fabric/events.jsonl (line 430), appendEditCounter -> .fabric/.cache/edit-counter (line 474), appendHintSilenceCounter -> .fabric/.cache/hint-silence-counter (line 504). This is exactly the multi-window scenario for which injections.jsonl received the O_EXCL advisory lock in commit 76bc350 (injection-log.cjs:33 appendLockedLine), but that fix was NOT applied here. Two client sessions editing the same repo can interleave partial writes and corrupt a JSONL line in events.jsonl (which the server then either silently drops or flags as partial_write_at_tail) and corrupt the edit-counter / silence-counter sidecars that drive doctor lint #26 and the archive-cadence Signal A. Of the three, events.jsonl corruption is the most damaging because that ledger is load-bearing for cite-coverage and archive heuristics.
fix_direction: Route all three appends through the appendLockedLine pattern (factor it out of injection-log.cjs into lib/ and share it), or at minimum events.jsonl since it is the load-bearing ledger. The counters could alternatively be made session-scoped (filename suffixed with session_id, like hint-dismiss-{sessionId}.json) so concurrent writers never touch the same file.

context.notes: Discovered by reliability perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by worktree/issue-sweep-main-migration after selectively porting issue-sweep implementation.

resolution: Resolved by routing PreToolUse narrow hook shared-file appends through appendLockedLine for events.jsonl, edit-counter, and hint-silence-counter; verified by hook-append-locked, edit-intent-ledger, and knowledge-hint-narrow tests.

title: Stop / SessionStart hooks append to events.jsonl + metrics.jsonl with bare appendFileSync — advisory lock not applied
description: The same un-serialized bare-append pattern exists in the Stop hook (fabric-hint.cjs) and SessionStart broad hook. emitSignalFiredEvent appends to events.jsonl at fabric-hint.cjs:1275, the assistant-turn observer appends per-turn at fabric-hint.cjs:1485, the empty-shell metrics fold appends to metrics.jsonl at fabric-hint.cjs:1510, and the broad hook appends a hook_surface_emitted event at knowledge-hint-broad.cjs:968. These are concurrent hook processes (Stop / SessionStart fire across multiple windows) appending to the SAME shared ledger files with no lock. Under the documented multi-window constraint, two simultaneous Stop fires (or a Stop racing a PreToolUse append from finding #1) can interleave and corrupt a line. The append-without-lock is the same class of defect that 76bc350 fixed for injections.jsonl but left unfixed on the events.jsonl / metrics.jsonl writers.
fix_direction: Wrap these ledger appends in the shared appendLockedLine advisory-lock helper. Since multiple distinct files are written from hooks, generalize the lock helper to take a target path.

context.notes: Discovered by reliability perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by worktree/issue-sweep-main-migration after selectively porting issue-sweep implementation.

title: KnowledgeIdAllocator.allocate() read-modify-write of agents.meta.json counters is not serialized — concurrent allocation yields duplicate stable_ids
description: allocate() performs readMeta() -> allocateKnowledgeId(advance counter) -> writeMetaAtomic() as three separate awaited steps with no lock or queue (knowledge-id-allocator.ts:49-56). atomicWriteJson only guarantees readers never see a torn file; it does NOT guarantee the read-modify-write is atomic. Two concurrent windows running fabric-review approve / fab_review at the same time (review.ts:557 and review.ts:895 both call allocator.allocate) can each read counter=N, each compute id N+1, and each write back N+1 — minting DUPLICATE stable_ids and clobbering each other's counter advance. Unlike the event-ledger, which at least funnels through an in-process LedgerWriteQueue (atomic-write.ts:75), the allocator has no serialization at all, and even the queue is in-process only (atomic-write.ts:62-63 explicitly: 'Does NOT provide cross-process locking') so cross-window review is unprotected. Duplicate stable_ids violate the allocator's stated uniqueness invariant (knowledge-id-allocator.ts:39) and corrupt KB identity.
fix_direction: Guard the read-modify-write with a cross-process advisory lock on agents.meta.json (O_EXCL lock-file like injection-log.cjs, or proper-lockfile). Loop-based promotion in review.ts amplifies the window, so the lock should ideally span the whole batch or be re-acquired per allocate.

context.notes: Discovered by reliability perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by retiring project-local KnowledgeIdAllocator/agents.meta counters; stable_id allocation now uses write-target store counters.json guarded by withFileLock in allocateStoreKnowledgeId. Verified by store-counters and review tests.

resolution: Resolved by retiring project-local KnowledgeIdAllocator/agents.meta counters; stable_id allocation now uses write-target store counters.json guarded by withFileLock in allocateStoreKnowledgeId. Verified by store-counters and review tests.

title: Corrupt agents.meta.json silently falls back to empty meta, and the next allocate() atomically overwrites the file — destroys all node entries
description: readMeta() catches a JSON.parse failure on agents.meta.json and returns { revision: '', nodes: {} } as 'fresh meta' (knowledge-id-allocator.ts:84-87). The comment claims this avoids 'corrupting further', but allocate() then does writeMetaAtomic({ ...meta, counters: nextCounters }) (line 54) — atomically writing back that near-empty object. So a transiently/partially-corrupt meta (e.g. one truncated by a concurrent non-atomic writer, or a half-written file observed mid-rename on some filesystems) is silently REPLACED with a meta that has revision='', nodes={}, and a counter that restarts effectively from zero-context. All previously-registered knowledge node entries are wiped, and counters can regress (since the corrupt file's real counter value was discarded), re-issuing already-used stable_ids. The silent fallback masks the corruption instead of surfacing it.
fix_direction: On JSON.parse failure do NOT proceed to overwrite. Either throw (let doctor surface + repair), or preserve the corrupt file to a .corrupted sidecar (as event-ledger.ts:307 does for ledgers) before any rewrite, and refuse to allocate until reconciled.

context.notes: Discovered by reliability perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by retiring the corrupt agents.meta allocator read path; writeKnowledgeMeta is a store-only compatibility no-op and id minting no longer overwrites agents.meta from a corrupt parse fallback. Verified by store-counters, review, and doctor store_counter_drift tests.

resolution: Resolved by retiring the corrupt agents.meta allocator read path; writeKnowledgeMeta is a store-only compatibility no-op and id minting no longer overwrites agents.meta from a corrupt parse fallback. Verified by store-counters, review, and doctor store_counter_drift tests.

title: Server-side metrics.jsonl and cite-rollup.jsonl appends bypass the ledger write queue (no serialization, no fsync)
description: flushMetrics appends to .fabric/metrics.jsonl with a raw appendFile (metrics.ts:102) and appendCiteRollupRow appends to .fabric/cite-rollup.jsonl with a raw appendFile (cite-rollup.ts:40). Neither goes through createLedgerWriteQueue / runExclusive (the serialization the event-ledger uses, event-ledger.ts:189). Within a single MCP process this is usually fine (one writer), but multiple MCP server instances (one per client window — the user runs concurrent sessions, and each client can spawn its own server endpoint) appending to the same repo's metrics.jsonl can interleave. metrics.ts at least re-seeds the bucket on failure (line 104) so counts survive, but a torn JSONL line still poisons the file for readers. cite-rollup has no such recovery.
fix_direction: Route both through the shared ledgerQueue.append (or the same instance used by event-ledger), and consider the advisory-lock helper for the cross-process case. cite-rollup in particular should not lose un-rolled-up turns on a torn write.

context.notes: Discovered by reliability perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by worktree/issue-sweep-main-migration after selectively porting issue-sweep implementation.

resolution: Resolved by routing metrics.jsonl and cite-rollup.jsonl server appends through createLedgerWriteQueue append serialization; verified by metrics and cite-rollup serialization tests.

title: Hook sidecar state writes (shown-cache, last-emit, state-store) are non-atomic plain writeFileSync — crash/concurrency can truncate the file
description: writeJsonState / writeTextState use plain writeFileSync with no tmp-file+rename (state-store.cjs:49, 70). Same for the shown-cache write at fabric-hint.cjs:996 and the maintenance last-emit timestamp at fabric-hint.cjs:1134. A crash or concurrent write mid-writeFileSync leaves a truncated/garbled file. The blast radius is bounded: readers wrap JSON.parse in try/catch and return null on failure (state-store.cjs:40, fabric-hint.cjs read paths), so a corrupt cache degrades to 'counter reset' rather than a crash, and the module documents counter loss as acceptable (state-store.cjs:21). Still, the shown-cache (fabric-hint.cjs:982, NOT session-scoped) and edit-counter being shared-not-per-session means concurrent windows write the same file; atomic write would remove the truncation window entirely.
fix_direction: Use write-to-tmp + rename for these sidecar writes (mirror atomic-write.ts semantics in a small cjs helper). Lower priority because the read path already degrades gracefully to null, but it removes the corruption window for the non-session-scoped caches.

title: Two divergent `extractBody` implementations with different semantics
description: Frontmatter-stripping logic exists as two separate functions named identically but behaving differently. `knowledge-sections.ts:90` does NOT trim and only strips BOM (`return content.slice(match[0].length)` / `content.replace(/^\uFEFF/u, "")`). `review.ts:255` DOES `.trim()` the result and uses a slightly different regex (no capture group). Same name + same conceptual job + different output is a latent bug: a caller migrating between the two paths silently gets trailing/leading-whitespace-differing bodies. review.ts re-declares the helper privately instead of importing the shared one.
fix_direction: Delete the private copy in review.ts and import `extractBody` from knowledge-sections.ts. Reconcile the trim() difference deliberately (pick one contract — likely trim at the API boundary, not in the shared stripper) and add a test pinning the chosen behavior.

title: doctor.ts is a 10,117-line god-file (102 functions, 51 inspect* + 51 create*Check)
description: packages/server/src/services/doctor.ts is 10,117 lines — over 2x the next-largest source file (review.ts 1,479). It holds 51 `inspect*` collectors, 51 matching `create*Check` builders, 7 `apply*` fixers, plus the orchestrators `runDoctorReport` (~364 lines, 1100-1463), `runDoctorFix` (~334 lines, 1477-1810), `runDoctorApplyLint` (1810+). Each lint check follows an identical inspect→create boilerplate shape (see createDraftBacklogCheck 3977-4000, createDriftUnconsumedCheck 4043+), so the file grows linearly with every new rc check and is the single highest-churn maintenance surface. Navigation, review, and merge-conflict risk are all amplified.
fix_direction: Split into per-domain check modules (e.g. checks/knowledge-lifecycle.ts, checks/bootstrap-drift.ts, checks/skill-budget.ts) each exporting an {inspect, createCheck} pair conforming to a shared `DoctorCheckModule` interface; have runDoctorReport iterate a registry array instead of hand-wiring 51 calls. Pre-1.0 + clean-slate-preferred owner makes this a low-risk mechanical extraction.

title: Scan/recommendation logic forked across cli and server-http-experimental with inconsistent language
description: Project-scan recommendation building lives in two places that have diverged. packages/server-http-experimental/src/api/scan.ts:157-177 emits English keyed strings ("L0: Run `fabric install`...", "L1: Add tech-stack TODOs..."). packages/cli/src/scanner/forensic.ts:1428-1448 emits hardcoded Simplified-Chinese recommendations ("建议向用户确认 Cocos Creator..."). Both consume the same shared detector but produce parallel, drifting recommendation sets — neither routes through the i18n layer (shared/i18n/locales) the rest of the codebase uses, so language is inconsistent with the locale-driven `fabric_language` config.
fix_direction: Route both recommendation builders through shared/i18n keys (the cli.scan.recommendation.* namespace already exists in en.ts/zh-CN.ts:1070+). Extract a single shared buildScanRecommendations() in shared so cli and the experimental HTTP server cannot drift.

title: knip.config.ts carries a stale workspace-resolution workaround pending 1.8.x
description: knip.config.ts ignoreDependencies block disables knip checking for workspace packages + runtime deps (express, chokidar, minimatch, picocolors, etc.) with the comment `TODO: review during 1.8.x patch when knip workspace resolution matures`. This is a broad suppression that masks any genuinely-unused runtime dependency going forward, and the TODO is an open maintenance debt with no owner/date. knip's own `Configuration hints (30)` output flags several of these entries as removable (e.g. `@fenglimg/fabric-shared`, `@clack/prompts`), suggesting the workaround is already partly stale.
fix_direction: Action the existing TODO: re-test knip without the workspace ignoreDependencies entries (knip already reports 30 config hints saying several are removable). Trim the ignore list to only entries that still genuinely false-positive, restoring real unused-dependency detection.

title: Vector retrieval embeds the ENTIRE candidate corpus per request — no persisted embedding index
description: When embeddings are enabled, plan-context.ts:311-315 calls buildVectorScores over `rawItems.map(...)` — i.e. EVERY non-suppressed KB entry. buildVectorScores (vector-retrieval.ts:155) does `embedder.embed([queryText, ...items.map(item => item.text)])`, CPU-embedding the query plus all N candidate documents on EVERY plan-context/recall call. There is NO persisted/cached embedding index (the header comment at bm25.ts:11-13 explicitly notes 'no index to persist' for BM25, and the vector path inherits the same per-call rebuild). As the KB grows to hundreds/thousands of entries, each recall re-embeds the whole corpus on CPU — embedding cost is the dominant term and scales linearly with KB size per request. This is the single most growth-sensitive operation once vectors are turned on.
fix_direction: Persist per-entry embeddings keyed by stable_id + content hash (e.g. in agents.meta.json or a sidecar), embed only the query + any entries whose hash changed since last index build. Embedding the full corpus per request defeats the purpose of a cache and makes vectors unusable past a few hundred entries.

context.notes: Discovered by scalability perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by bounded document vector cache that embeds only query plus cache-miss docs; verified by vector-retrieval tests.

resolution: Resolved by bounded document vector cache that embeds only query plus cache-miss docs; verified by vector-retrieval tests.

title: BM25 model rebuilt from scratch over the full corpus on every query-bearing plan-context call
description: sortDescriptionItems (plan-context.ts:551-555) calls buildBm25Model over `rawItems.map(... tokenize(documentTextForItem(...)))` for every plan-context call that carries query terms. buildBm25Model (bm25.ts:45-99) tokenizes every document, builds per-term documentFrequency and per-doc termFreq maps — O(corpus tokens) per request. The header (bm25.ts:11-14) justifies this as 'corpus is small (tens of entries)'. That assumption is the scaling cliff: the KB is explicitly designed to GROW (the whole project is a knowledge accumulator), and once it reaches hundreds/thousands of entries every recall re-tokenizes and re-indexes the entire corpus. Tokenization additionally happens twice for the same text — once in buildVectorScores' documentTextForItem path and once here — though that is a constant factor.
fix_direction: Memoize the BM25 model (and the tokenization of each document) per revision_hash so it is built once per KB change rather than once per request; invalidate on meta revision change (same trigger as contextCache).

context.notes: Discovered by scalability perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by corpus-keyed BM25 model cache and prebuilt scoring context; verified by plan-context BM25 cache tests.

resolution: Resolved by corpus-keyed BM25 model cache and prebuilt scoring context; verified by plan-context BM25 cache tests.

title: Event-ledger rotation is age-only (30d retention), never size-bounded — file grows unbounded within the retention window under heavy usage
description: rotateEventLedgerIfNeeded (event-ledger.ts:386-511) partitions strictly by `ts < now - retentionDays*86400000` (line 395, 441). retentionDays defaults to 30 (line 27) with config options of only 7/30/90 (line 637). There is NO size-based trigger: a high-frequency workspace (the audit comment at read-ledger.ts:101-105 notes edit_intent_checked 'fires per affected_path on every Edit/Write/MultiEdit') can append far more than 50MB of events WITHIN 30 days, and rotation will keep all of them because they are younger than the cutoff. The 50MB threshold (event-ledger.ts:28, 200) only emits a one-shot stderr warning — it does NOT trigger rotation. Combined with the full-file-read in readEventLedger, the in-window growth degrades every ledger read. Rotation also only runs on a 6h server tick (rotation-tick.ts:18,40) or doctor --fix (doctor.ts:1677); short-lived per-session MCP spawns that exit before 6h never rotate.
fix_direction: Add a size-based rotation trigger (rotate oldest N% when file exceeds payloadHardBytes-style ceiling regardless of age) and run a startup rotation tick (currently first tick fires only after a full 6h interval per rotation-tick.ts:30-31). Tie the 50MB warn threshold to an actual rotate, not just stderr.

context.notes: Discovered by scalability perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

title: readLedger merges legacy ledger + full event-ledger projection on every rehydrate, double full-file reads
description: readLedger (read-ledger.ts:56-69) runs readLegacyLedger (full read+parse of audit.jsonl, lines 71-91) AND readLedgerFromEventLedger (read-ledger.ts:186-210) in parallel; the latter calls readEventLedger() with NO filter (line 187) — a full events.jsonl read — then projects every edit_intent_checked event into a grouped Map (lines 188-209). source/since filters are applied only AFTER the full merge (lines 66-68). rehydrate-state.ts:65 calls this on state rehydration. So each rehydrate pays two full-file reads (legacy + events) plus a full grouping pass that scales with total event count, not with the entries actually needed.
fix_direction: Push source/since filters into the event read (event_type='edit_intent_checked' + since) and stream; avoid re-reading the whole events.jsonl whenever a bounded slice is needed.

title: selectionTokenCache is an unbounded in-memory Map with no eviction — grows with session/call count
description: createSelectionToken (plan-context.ts:447-457) inserts into the module-level `selectionTokenCache = new Map()` (plan-context.ts:143) on EVERY plan-context call, with a default 30-minute TTL (line 140, bumped from 5min in rc.37). Entries are only deleted lazily on readSelectionToken when already expired (plan-context.ts:424-426) — there is NO periodic sweep. A long-lived MCP server handling many sessions/recalls accumulates one never-read-again token per plan-context call for up to 30 minutes each; tokens whose getKnowledgeSections follow-up never arrives are never proactively evicted. Memory grows O(plan-context calls in any 30-min window). Bounded by TTL so not catastrophic, but unbounded in call-rate.
fix_direction: Add a periodic sweep (or LRU cap) for selectionTokenCache so expired tokens are reclaimed without waiting for a matching read that may never come; cap total entries for high-throughput servers.

context.notes: Discovered by scalability perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by bounded LRU selectionTokenCache with proactive expiry sweep; verified by selection-token-cache tests.

resolution: Resolved by bounded LRU selectionTokenCache with proactive expiry sweep; verified by selection-token-cache tests.

title: findStoreExecutableViolations does a synchronous unbounded recursive readdirSync+statSync walk of the entire store tree
description: findStoreExecutableViolations (store-disk-reader.ts:72-107) recursively walks every directory (readdirSync, line 76) and statSync's every entry (line 90) in a mounted store tree with no depth/file-count bound. As stores grow (the north-star is N parallel git stores, each an accumulating KB) this synchronous walk scales O(total files per store) and blocks the event loop during mount/verify. It excludes .git (line 83) but nothing else. Currently a mount-time / verify-time cost (not per-recall), so lower severity, but it runs per store and the store model is explicitly designed for growth in both store count and per-store size.
fix_direction: Make the scan async (fs/promises) and/or bound it (depth/file-count cap with a diagnostic when exceeded); cache results per store commit so re-mounts of an unchanged store skip the full walk.

title: stable_id counter width is fixed at NNNN (min 4 digits) — overflow semantics undefined past 9999 per type per store
description: The stable_id grammar is `K[PT]-TYPE-\d{4,}` (store-stable-id.ts:51 GLOBAL_REF_PATTERN uses `\d{4,}`; the local form reuses StableIdSchema). The `{4,}` quantifier technically allows more than 4 digits, so the regex itself does not hard-cap at 9999 — but the human-facing format and per-store/per-type counters (storeCountersSchema, store-stable-id.ts:107-127) are nonnegative integers with no documented rollover or width-expansion handling. Per store, per layer (KP/KT), per type (MOD/DEC/GLD/PIT/PRO) the counter is independent, so 9999 is a long way off for any single bucket; risk is low and only matters for a single extremely prolific store+type combination. Worth noting that the format string assumes 4 digits for alignment/sorting — lexicographic sort of stable_ids breaks once a counter crosses into 5 digits (K-DEC-9999 vs K-DEC-10000 sort wrong), and plan-context uses stable_id.localeCompare as a tiebreaker (plan-context.ts:715).
fix_direction: Confirm counter allocation zero-pads to a consistent width (and widens cleanly past 9999) so lexicographic stable_id tiebreaks in plan-context stay correct; document the rollover behavior. Low priority unless a single store+type bucket is expected to exceed ~10k entries.

context.notes: Discovered by scalability perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

resolution: Resolved by numeric-aware stable_id comparison via Intl numeric collation; verified by compare-stable-ids tests.

title: FabricError.actionHint infrastructure is entirely dead in the CLI — every CLI error shows only a bare message with no remedy
description: The whole structured-error stack (FabricError with a REQUIRED non-empty actionHint, packages/shared/src/errors/fabric-error.ts:10-14) plus the dedicated renderer renderFabricError/hasActionHint (packages/cli/src/lib/error-render.ts) exists to print '<message>\n  -> <actionHint>' to stderr. But grep across packages/cli/src shows ZERO throws of any FabricError subclass and ZERO call sites for renderFabricError or hasActionHint outside error-render.ts itself — it is dead code. All 40 CLI error throws are plain `throw new Error(...)` which propagate to citty's runMain default handler (packages/cli/src/index.ts:21-22), and citty prints only err.message, never the actionHint field (the renderer's own header comment at error-render.ts:1-8 documents exactly this drop). Net effect: the entire remedy-hint UX the team built for CLI failures never reaches the terminal; users see one-line messages with no 'how to fix'.
fix_direction: Either (a) wrap runMain in index.ts with a try/catch that calls renderFabricError(err) + process.exit(1) when hasActionHint(err), or (b) migrate the plain throws to FabricError subclasses AND wire the renderer. Pick one; right now the actionHint contract is enforced at construction but never surfaced.

context.notes: Discovered by ux perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by CLI top-level FabricError actionHint renderer; verified by error-render tests.

resolution: Resolved by CLI top-level FabricError actionHint renderer; verified by error-render tests.

title: git clone runs with stdout+progress fully suppressed — `fabric install --global <url>` is silent for the entire clone of a remote store
description: gitClone invokes `git clone url dest` with stdio: ['ignore','ignore','pipe'] (packages/cli/src/install/run-global-install.ts:38). Both git's normal progress bar (stderr) is hidden and stdout is discarded. For a large remote shared store this means `fabric install --global <url>` appears frozen with no feedback during the network clone. No spinner or progress line is emitted around the call (line 47).
fix_direction: Show a clack spinner ('Cloning store from <url>…') around gitClone, or let git's stderr progress through (stdio inherit/pipe-with-passthrough), so the user knows the clone is running.

title: git failures swallow git's own stderr — user sees generic 'Command failed' instead of the real cause (auth/dirty-tree/etc.)
description: In defaultPull (packages/cli/src/sync/run-sync.ts:91-111) git pull --rebase captures stdout+stderr, classifies CONFLICT and offline patterns, but for ANY other failure (auth denied, detached HEAD, dirty working tree, permission) it does `throw error` at line 110 — discarding the captured `error.stderr` detail it just read at line 99. The user gets execFileSync's default 'Command failed: git pull --rebase' with no git diagnostic. Same pattern in run-global-install.ts gitClone (line 38) where stderr is piped/captured but never surfaced on failure.
fix_direction: On the rethrow path, attach/print the captured git stderr (the `detail` string already computed) so the user sees git's actual error message instead of a generic shell failure.

title: Five CLI commands bypass i18n entirely — output stays English even when fabric_language is zh-CN
description: commands/whoami.ts, store.ts, scope-explain.ts, sync.ts, and metrics.ts do not import the i18n `t` translator at all (verified: none import from ../i18n). Their user-facing output is hardcoded English, e.g. whoami.ts:11 'no global Fabric config — run `fabric install --global <url>` first', store.ts:26 '(no stores mounted)', sync.ts:18 '...push deferred; re-run `fabric sync` when online', metrics.ts:37 '--since: invalid duration...'. The project's fabric_language: zh-CN preference (.fabric/fabric-config.json) does not affect any of these surfaces.
fix_direction: Route these strings through the i18n catalog (en.ts/zh-CN.ts) like install/doctor do. Note: even if kept English, prefer the projectRoot-aware getDoctorTranslator-style resolution so they honor fabric_language.

context.notes: Discovered by ux perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

title: Module-level translator uses OS env locale, not the project's fabric_language — non-doctor commands ignore configured language
description: packages/cli/src/i18n.ts:14-15 binds the exported `t` to detectNodeLocale() (env-driven). Only doctor gets a projectRoot-aware translator via getDoctorTranslator(projectRoot) (i18n.ts:17-19, the only non-self caller per grep). So install/uninstall/config/onboard-coverage — which DO import the module-level `t` — render in the OS locale rather than the repo's `.fabric/fabric-config.json` fabric_language. A user who set fabric_language: zh-CN on an en_US machine still sees English install/config output. The comment at i18n.ts:8-13 acknowledges this is intentional scoping but it contradicts the 'i18n is via fabric_language' product principle for everything except doctor.
fix_direction: Generalize the projectRoot-aware translator (resolveFabricLocale) to all commands that operate inside an installed project, falling back to detectNodeLocale only when no .fabric/fabric-config.json exists (e.g. pre-install).

title: Codebase forensic scan during `fabric install` has no progress feedback
description: buildForensicReport(target) (called at packages/cli/src/commands/install.ts:846) recursively walks the filesystem (scanner/forensic.ts:234 readdirSync recursion) and lazily loads web-tree-sitter parsers (forensic.ts:63-71, 170-172) to classify the project. The scanner emits no progress output (grep for console/log/spinner/progress in forensic.ts returns nothing), so on a large repo the install wizard appears to hang between the target and plan steps.

title: MCP 'selection_token is missing or expired' error gives the AI no recovery instruction
description: getKnowledgeSections throws a plain Error 'selection_token is missing or expired' (packages/server/src/services/knowledge-sections.ts:104) which surfaces to the AI client raw. Unlike the payload warnings in these tools (which all carry an action_hint, e.g. plan-context.ts:106, recall.ts:94, knowledge-sections.ts:64), this hard error tells the AI nothing about the remedy: re-run fab_plan_context to mint a fresh selection_token before retrying. The AGENTS.md rule states the token must come from the most recent fab_plan_context, but the error message itself does not, so the model has to already know that to recover.
fix_direction: Append the recovery hint to the message: '...expired — call fab_plan_context again to obtain a fresh selection_token, then retry'. Consider returning it as a structured McpToolError with actionHint rather than a bare Error.

title: doctor TL;DR ranked summary drops the actionHint that the full list shows
description: doctor's full issue list renders each finding with its remediation arrow ('  → <actionHint>', packages/cli/src/commands/doctor.ts:668-672), but the TL;DR top-N summary at the head of the output (doctor.ts:684-710) pushes only {severity, code, message} and truncates message to 140 chars (line 710) with no actionHint. A user who reads only the TL;DR (its purpose) sees the problem codes but not how to fix them, having to scroll to the full list. (Note: this is the one gap — the underlying check coverage is strong: all 66 issueCheck() error/warning calls in packages/server/src/services/doctor.ts pass a localized actionHint, so doctor lint codes are otherwise well-documented.)

title: doctor human output uses Unicode arrow → in live stdout, contradicting the codebase's own GBK-safe ASCII '-> ' convention
description: lib/error-render.ts:49 documents a deliberate rule: action-hint arrows use ASCII '-> ' (not Unicode →) for GBK terminal compatibility, and renderFabricError() at error-render.ts:55 follows it. But the doctor human renderer emits raw Unicode '→' directly to stdout in three live output paths: the folded maintainer-hint breadcrumb (commands/doctor.ts:670), the action-hint line (commands/doctor.ts:672), and the TL;DR severity-order legend 'fixable→manual→warn' (commands/doctor.ts:700). On a non-UTF-8 / GBK or legacy terminal these render as mojibake; for a screen reader the bare arrow glyph is also less meaningful than 'then'/'->'. The fix is mechanical and the project already decided which way is correct.
fix_direction: Replace the Unicode '→' in the three writeStdout() call sites (doctor.ts:670, 672, 700) with the ASCII '-> ' arrow used by lib/error-render.ts:55, matching the GBK-compatibility convention documented at error-render.ts:49.

title: Color gating honors NO_COLOR but ignores FORCE_COLOR, so users piping output cannot opt back into symbols/color
description: isColorEnabled() in packages/cli/src/colors.ts:6-8 returns true only when NO_COLOR is unset AND both stdout and stderr are TTYs. This correctly disables color when piping (good) and respects the NO_COLOR opt-out. However it never checks FORCE_COLOR, the complementary informal-standard env var that lets a user force colorized/symbol output through a pager that does interpret ANSI (e.g. `fabric doctor | less -R`) or in CI logs. Because color-disabled also swaps the unicode status glyphs for ASCII text fallbacks (✓ → [ok], colors.ts:41-48), a user who pipes deliberately loses the richer rendering with no way to re-enable it. NO_COLOR support without FORCE_COLOR support is the asymmetric half of the de-facto standard.
fix_direction: Extend isColorEnabled() to honor FORCE_COLOR: when process.env.FORCE_COLOR is set to a truthy value, enable color/symbols regardless of TTY (while still letting NO_COLOR win as the explicit opt-out). Mirrors picocolors' / chalk's own precedence.

context.notes: Discovered by accessibility perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by FORCE_COLOR support with NO_COLOR precedence; verified by colors-force-color tests.

resolution: Resolved by FORCE_COLOR support with NO_COLOR precedence; verified by colors-force-color tests.

title: HTML prototype: interactive controls lack accessible names (SVG-only buttons, fake search input, placeholder anchors)
description: ui-prototype-hybrid.html is a standalone dual-theme mockup (title 'Fabric v2 - Hybrid Dual-Theme UI'), not part of the shipped CLI/MCP product, so impact is limited unless it is promoted to a real UI. Within it: the sidebar nav links are all href="#" placeholders with inline SVGs that have no aria-label/title (lines 86-109), so a screen reader announces only the trailing text node and the icons are unlabeled decoration with no role; the header 'Search path...' control is a non-interactive <div> styled as an input (lines 135-141) rather than a real <input> with a <label>, so it is not reachable or announced as a search field; the 'Copy' button's leading SVG (line 261) and the status-dot+text patterns rely on adjacent text (which is fine), but the copy/theme icon-buttons mix labeled and unlabeled patterns inconsistently. The theme-toggle button does set title="Toggle Theme" (line 148), which is the correct pattern the others should follow.
fix_direction: If this prototype is ever shipped: give each decorative SVG aria-hidden="true" and add aria-label (or visible text) to icon-only controls; replace the fake search <div> (lines 135-141) with a real <input type="search"> + associated <label> (or aria-label); give the nav anchors real hrefs/roles. If it remains a throwaway mockup, no action needed.

title: fabric install scaffolds per-project ledgers (events.jsonl, metrics.jsonl, cite-rollup.jsonl) into .fabric/ but never writes a .gitignore to exclude them
description: `fabric install` creates `.fabric/events.jsonl` in the target project (install.ts:828, listed as a created artifact at install.ts:1766) and the hooks/MCP server append metrics.jsonl and cite-rollup.jsonl alongside it. No code path in packages/cli/src writes a project-level .gitignore or .git/info/exclude entry for these files (grep for 'gitignore' across packages/cli/src returns zero install-time writers; only the STORE scaffolder writes a .gitignore, core.ts:76, and it covers state/ + .cache/ only). This monorepo itself avoids committing the ledgers solely because its hand-maintained ROOT .gitignore lists them (.gitignore: .fabric/events.jsonl, .fabric/metrics.jsonl, .fabric/cite-rollup.jsonl). A downstream user who runs `fabric install` in their repo gets NO such protection: events.jsonl records target_paths (file paths) and free-text intent fields and will be swept into version control on the next `git add .`, leaking one developer's edit/activity trail and any pasted snippets to the whole team via the shared repo.
fix_direction: On install, append `.fabric/events.jsonl`, `.fabric/metrics.jsonl`, `.fabric/cite-rollup.jsonl`, `.fabric/events.archive/`, `.fabric/.cache/` to the project's .gitignore (idempotently), or write `.fabric/.gitignore` covering the volatile ledgers, mirroring the STORE_GITIGNORE pattern already used for stores.

context.notes: Discovered by compliance perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by worktree/issue-sweep-main-migration after selectively porting issue-sweep implementation.

resolution: Resolved by scaffolded .fabric/.gitignore covering events.jsonl, metrics.jsonl, cite-rollup.jsonl, injections.jsonl, .cache/, and *.lock; verified by init-atomic gitignore scaffold test.

title: events.jsonl append path has no secret-scan / redaction; credential-shaped strings in free-text intent/message fields are persisted verbatim at rest
description: A secret scanner exists (shared/src/store/secret-scan.ts: AWS keys, private-key blocks, openai/github/slack tokens, credential assignments) and is correctly enforced on the knowledge-entry write path (extract-knowledge.ts:298 calls hasSecrets and blocks the write) and on debug-bundle output (observability.ts redacts). But the event-ledger append path applies NO secret scan — appendEventLedgerEvent (event-ledger.ts:169) only truncates strings to 4KB (truncateLongStrings, line 156). The editIntentCheckedEvent.intent (event-ledger schema line 75), knowledge_context_planned.intent (line 40), and mcp_event.message (line 115, z.unknown) are free-text fields populated from AI/human input — a pasted command line, stack trace, or git URL with an embedded token lands unredacted in .fabric/events.jsonl. Combined with finding #1 (ledger not gitignored in user projects) this becomes a committed-secret vector. Severity medium rather than high because the file is intended to be local and secrets land there only via user paste, not by the tool harvesting env/tokens.
fix_direction: Run redactSecrets() (already exported from shared) over string fields inside truncateLongStrings or just before the eventLedgerEventSchema.parse in appendEventLedgerEvent, so credential-shaped content is redacted before it is written to disk — same control already applied to debug-bundle output.

context.notes: Discovered by compliance perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

title: Git remote URL stored verbatim in tracked store.json; a credential-embedded URL would be committed/pushed with the store
description: Store registration persists the user-supplied remote string verbatim into store.json (store-ops.ts:91 `{ store_uuid, alias, remote: options.remote }`; store-lifecycle.ts:72 `remote: string | null`). store.json is a tracked file in each store's own git repo — it is NOT listed in STORE_GITIGNORE (core.ts:36, which only excludes state/, agents.meta.json, .cache/). If a user supplies a remote of the form `https://user:token@host/repo.git`, that credential is written into a committed file and would be pushed/shared along with the store. No sanitization of userinfo is applied to the remote before persistence. Low severity: requires the user to pass a credential-laden URL (an anti-pattern), and the normal path sets the auth-bearing URL only in .git/config (gitignored by git itself), not the store.json registry copy.
fix_direction: Strip userinfo (user:pass@) from the remote URL before persisting it to store.json (store only the scheme/host/path locator), or run the remote string through redactSecrets/a URL-credential strip on write.

context.notes: Discovered by compliance perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by scrubbing credential userinfo before persisting mounted store remotes and suggested remotes; verified by store-lifecycle tests.

resolution: Resolved by scrubbing credential userinfo before persisting mounted store remotes and suggested remotes; verified by store-lifecycle tests.

title: Personal-layer (KP-*) stable_ids embed a uid = sha256(git user.email); pseudonymous identifier travels in any shared cross-reference to a personal entry
description: deriveUid (cli/src/store/uid.ts:11) runs `git config user.email`, lowercases it and stores sha256(email)[:12] as the uid (no salt). This uid is embedded into personal-store stable_ids as a namespace segment: global_ref format `<store_uuid>:<uid>:<local_id>` (shared/src/schemas/store-stable-id.ts:24). The hash is one-way and unsalted but deterministic — the same email always yields the same uid, so it is a stable pseudonymous identifier (re-identifiable by anyone who can hash a candidate email list). The read-set resolver isolates personal stores correctly (store-resolver.ts:51 excludes personal stores from required_stores matching; line 82-85 adds the personal store ONLY to the local user's own read set) so personal KP-* entries do not leak into another developer's shared recall by default. Residual low-severity exposure: if a personal entry is ever cited/cross-referenced from a shared store, or a personal store is shared, the email-derived uid segment travels with the id.
fix_direction: Acceptable as-is for an OSS local tool. If hardening is wanted: salt the uid hash (per-machine random salt stored in global config) so the value is not a globally-correlatable email fingerprint, and document the uid derivation in SECURITY.md.

context.notes: Discovered by compliance perspective in DBP-20260530-214509

issue_history.note: Resolved by optional salted uid derivation while preserving backward-compatible default uid stability; verified by uid-salt tests.

resolution: Resolved by optional salted uid derivation while preserving backward-compatible default uid stability; verified by uid-salt tests.

title: Repeated full-corpus loading of event ledger into memory

It is called repeatedly (8+ times) across inspection functions like inspectEventLedger, inspectCiteGoodhart, buildDriftedStableIds, and inspectDriftUnconsumed. For large event ledgers this results in unbounded memory allocation and CPU overhead.
fix_direction: Parse the ledger once per doctor run and pass the resulting events array to all dependent inspection functions, or use a stream parser.

title: Race condition in event ledger truncation
description: truncateLedgerToLastNewline runs without a cross-process lock. If an active client session appends an event concurrently while doctor is truncating the ledger to fix a partial write, the concurrent write will be destroyed or cause further file corruption.

description: The PreToolUse hook (knowledge-hint-narrow.cjs) is invoked synchronously on every file edit tool (Edit, Write, MultiEdit) to inject narrow knowledge hints. title: Lock-Reclaim Race Condition and Lock Theft in withFileLock
description: withFileLock manages cross-process advisory locking via O_CREAT|O_EXCL. If a lock becomes stale (exceeding staleMs, e.g. 10s), another process reclaims it by unlink(lockPath) then open(lockPath,'wx'). When the original slow holder finally completes, its unconditional finally block calls unlink(lockPath), deleting the NEW holder's active lock file. This leaves the new holder unprotected, allowing concurrent entry into the critical section and shared-state corruption.
fix_direction: Write a unique process/session token (UUID) into the exclusive lock file on creation; in finally, only unlink if file contents match the current holder's token.

title: Personal-layer (KP-*) metadata leaks into git-committed agents.meta.json
description: buildKnowledgeMeta scans both team-root (.fabric/knowledge/) and personal-root (~/.fabric/knowledge/), merges KT-* and KP-* nodes into one dictionary, and writes .fabric/agents.meta.json in the project root. Since that file is NOT gitignored, developers' personal-layer metadata (absolute home paths, titles, summaries, section headers) is permanently committed to the shared repo. Per-developer personal dirs also cause constant computeRevision mismatches, triggering drift/heal spam.
fix_direction: Exclude KP-* / ~/.fabric/knowledge entries from the shared .fabric/agents.meta.json; persist personal metadata machine-locally (.fabric/.cache/ or ~/.fabric/). Exclude personal entries from computeRevision.

title: Unconditional recursive workspace walk on every read/plan-context request
description: buildKnowledgeMeta is called unconditionally on all read/plan paths (loadActiveMeta / loadActiveMetaOrStale during planContext, recall, getKnowledgeSections). It triggers findFabricVerifyAnnotations -> findTestFiles, which recursively walks the ENTIRE workspace directory + stats all files to inspect annotations. At 10x-100x codebase size, a full-workspace traversal on every read request degrades performance catastrophically.
fix_direction: Cache the workspace test-file list or only re-walk on a file-mutation event from the watcher, instead of a full recursive walk per incoming API request.

title: Frontmatter YAML Injection in Modify/Approve/Reject Paths
description: During metadata update paths in review.ts, string arrays like tags and relevance_paths are joined with a bare ', ' separator without per-element double-quoting or escaping. If any tag or relevance path contains YAML special characters (']' or newlines), it can escape the array context and inject arbitrary YAML keys/values into the frontmatter block.
fix_direction: Use quoteRelevancePath / quoteIfNeeded on each element of tags and relevance_paths before joining.

title: Frontmatter Escape via Backslash in quoteIfNeeded (review.ts)
description: quoteIfNeeded in review.ts only replaces double-quotes with escaped double-quotes and does NOT escape backslashes first. A value ending in a backslash ('foo\') yields a trailing backslash escaping the closing quote, letting subsequent content forge arbitrary frontmatter keys. This is the same bug class as the known extract-knowledge.ts:718 issue, present in a separate un-fixed copy.
fix_direction: Escape backslashes first (replace(/\\/g,'\\\\')) before escaping double-quotes, matching quoteRelevancePath in extract-knowledge.ts.

title: Synchronous Repository Walks and File Reads in Doctor Checks
description: fabric doctor executes deep repository walks to lint the KB and system state. During loop processing it performs synchronous readdirSync, readFileSync, statSync to check all MD entries and session cache files. For large repos or slow storage this synchronous blocking I/O freezes the single-threaded Node loop, causing slow CLI responses and high CPU.
fix_direction: Refactor deep directory inspections and reads in doctor.ts to async readdir/stat/readFile and parallelize with Promise.all().

Verification: pnpm --filter @fenglimg/fabric-server build; pnpm --filter @fenglimg/fabric-server exec vitest run src/services/doctor.test.ts -t "enrichDescriptions" --reporter=dot; rg sync-fs check returned no synchronous fs APIs in doctor.ts.

title: Counter overwrite: writeKnowledgeMeta rebuilds without the allocator lock
description: KnowledgeIdAllocator.allocate increments stable_id counters in agents.meta.json inside a withFileLock critical section. But writeKnowledgeMeta (sync/auto-heal/doctor) reads + recomputes via buildKnowledgeMeta and writes back with atomicWriteText WITHOUT acquiring the lock. If it reads before allocate's incremented write lands, it overwrites the meta with stale counters, causing duplicate stable_id allocation and index collisions.
fix_direction: Acquire withFileLock on ${metaPath}.lock in writeKnowledgeMeta before reading/computing/writing the metadata, serializing rebuilds against allocator increments.

context.notes: Discovered by reliability perspective in DBP-20260531-102040 (engine=agy, round 1; dedup deferred per user)

title: Cross-process event loss in ledger rotation and drops
description: rotateEventLedgerIfNeeded and dropEventsFromLedger read events.jsonl, partition/filter, and overwrite via atomicWriteText, serialized only by an in-process queue (ledgerQueue.runExclusive). Across multiple client sessions or CLI/server processes the separate queues do not lock against each other, so one process's rewrite clobbers concurrent appends/rotations.
fix_direction: Wrap the critical sections of rotateEventLedgerIfNeeded and dropEventsFromLedger in withFileLock on events.jsonl.lock for cross-process mutual exclusion.

context.notes: Discovered by reliability perspective in DBP-20260531-102040 (engine=agy, round 1; dedup deferred per user)

issue_history.note: Resolved by worktree/issue-sweep-main-migration after selectively porting issue-sweep implementation.

resolution: Resolved by wrapping rotateEventLedgerIfNeeded and dropEventsFromLedger in exclusiveLedgerWrite, combining withFileLock on events.jsonl.lock with the in-process ledger queue; verified by event-ledger rotation/drop tests and shared atomic-write lock/queue tests.

title: Torn writes / page-cache corruption from omission of fsync
description: atomicWriteJson persists critical derived files like agents.meta.json but calls atomicWriteText without a truthy fsync option — write temp then rename, no flush. A power loss/crash right after rename can leave unflushed page-cache, corrupting metadata or producing a zero-byte agents.meta.json.
fix_direction: Enable fsync by default for critical metadata/config writes (agents.meta.json, settings.json) in KnowledgeIdAllocator and doctor fixes, forcing fd.datasync before rename.

context.notes: Discovered by reliability perspective in DBP-20260531-102040 (engine=agy, round 1; dedup deferred per user)

issue_history.note: Resolved by worktree/issue-sweep-main-migration after selectively porting issue-sweep implementation.

resolution: Resolved by making atomicWriteText fsync/datasync durable by default while preserving explicit fsync:false opt-out; verified by shared atomic-write unit/integration tests, server event-ledger rotation/drop tests, and shared package build.

title: Boilerplate Copy-Paste Installation Functions for Skills

Each manually reads a template, validates size, maps destinations, runs copyTextIdempotent, and logs. Violates DRY and risks drift when adding skills.

title: Swallowed cache write failures silently break hooks and nudge logic

description: In color-enabled terminals, status indicators are bare Unicode shapes (check/!/x) styled with ANSI color. Screen readers read these ambiguously (or skip them) and colorblind users lose the color channel, leaving only shape. Only NO_COLOR mode includes text fallback ([ok]/[warn]/[error]).

title: No PII scanning/redaction before capturing into KB or event ledger
description: secret-scan.ts only targets technical secrets (AWS/OpenAI/Slack/GitHub tokens, basic password assignments). There are no rules/heuristics to detect/redact PII (emails, IPs, names, phone numbers). Since event ledger inputs capture arbitrary caller free-text (intent, message, reason), any PII is permanently logged to plain-text events.jsonl or the KB without redaction.
fix_direction: Add dedicated PII regex rules (emails, IPs, phone numbers) into redactSecrets/scanForSecrets and apply across all telemetry logging and write-paths.

title: Audit gap: in-place knowledge edits (modifyEntry) emit no ledger event
description: When an in-place modification (title, summary, maturity, tags, relevance) is performed on a pending/canonical entry, reviewKnowledge dispatches to modifyEntry. Other actions (approve/reject/layer-flip/defer) emit telemetry to events.jsonl, but in-place edits write directly to frontmatter and exit WITHOUT a ledger event — allowing users or skills to silently change maturity levels or relevance paths.
fix_direction: Emit a knowledge_modified / knowledge_metadata_updated event in the modifyEntry in-place flow capturing stable_id/path, patch changes, original values, and session metadata.

resolution: Resolved by adding a knowledge_modified event for in-place fab_review modify actions with stable_id/path, changed_fields, before/after values, and reason; verified by shared build, event-ledger schema tests, and review service tests.

title: metrics.jsonl grows unbounded with NO rotation mechanism
description: Metric-natured events flush to .fabric/metrics.jsonl every 60s. Unlike events.jsonl (which has rotation + age cutoff), metrics.jsonl has NO rotation/age cutoff/truncation and appends indefinitely. readMetrics loads and parses the entire file into memory, causing linear memory/CPU degradation as it grows.
fix_direction: Add size-based and age-based rotation for metrics.jsonl like events.jsonl (archive old rows to a compressed sidecar, keep only active rows).

context.notes: Discovered by scalability perspective in DBP-20260531-102040 (engine=agy, round 1; dedup deferred per user)

issue_history.note: Resolved by worktree/issue-sweep-main-migration after selectively porting issue-sweep implementation.

resolution: Resolved by adding metrics.jsonl rotation with 30-day retention, size-based oldest-row eviction, archived rows under .fabric/metrics.archive, and best-effort post-flush size-triggered rotation.

title: Full-ledger in-memory scans for events and metrics files scale poorly
description: readEventLedger and readMetrics read their entire .jsonl into memory and split into lines, running JSON.parse + Zod validation per line. This happens during doctor health checks (inspectEventsJsonlGates on every CLI invocation/server loop), scaling O(M) with ledger size (up to 50MB before rotation), causing memory spikes and CPU choking.
fix_direction: Implement index/tail-based streaming scans (read only last N lines or since a timestamp) or a pre-aggregated in-memory index, avoiding full-text parse per call.

context.notes: Discovered by scalability perspective in DBP-20260531-102040 (engine=agy, round 1; dedup deferred per user)

issue_history.note: Resolved by worktree/issue-sweep-main-migration after selectively porting issue-sweep implementation.

resolution: Resolved for the remaining event/metrics ledger scan surfaces: readEventLedger now streams complete JSONL lines, rotateEventLedgerIfNeeded no longer read/splits the whole ledger, and readMetrics uses a createReadStream line scanner with no readFile/split path in metrics.ts.

title: Concurrent multi-window sessions cause cross-session selection-token invalidation
description: selectionTokenCache is a single global in-memory Map; tokens are bound to the project's revision_hash and cleared immediately on any file mutation. In multi-window concurrent sessions, one window modifying a knowledge file (sync or frontmatter edit) changes the revision hash, instantly invalidating active selection tokens in ALL other windows, aborting reasoning loops with 'selection_token is missing or expired'.
fix_direction: Keep active selection tokens valid for their full TTL (30 min) despite minor revision_hash mutations, or scope invalidation to the specific files mutated rather than globally destroying all active tokens.

context.notes: Discovered by scalability perspective in DBP-20260531-102040 (engine=agy, round 1; dedup deferred per user)

resolution: Regression coverage now proves selection tokens remain usable across read-set revision changes until TTL or LRU expiry; plan-context comments document that get_sections validates minted token state instead of globally invalidating on revision changes.

title: Synchronous blocking FS and process execution in multi-store core
description: initStore, listMarkdown, listStoreKnowledge, readKnowledgeAcrossStores, aggregatePendingAcrossStores use synchronous fs APIs (readdirSync, existsSync, mkdirSync, writeFileSync, readFileSync, unlinkSync) and synchronous execFileSync('git'). These block Node's single-threaded event loop; as store count or corpus grows or under concurrent sessions, the loop chokes, blocking all other HTTP/MCP requests.
fix_direction: Refactor multi-store core to async (fs.promises, execFile-as-promise) to prevent event-loop blockage during cross-store reads/writes or git interactions.

context.notes: Discovered by scalability perspective in DBP-20260531-102040 (engine=agy, round 1; dedup deferred per user)

issue_history.note: Converted multi-store core and production callers to async fs/promises + promisified git execution; verified shared/cli/server builds and focused store/cross-store tests.

resolution: multi-store core no longer imports blocking fs/process APIs: initStore, store project reads/writes, knowledge listing, cross-store recall/indexing, doctor scope lint, install/store command callers, and related tests now await async store operations.

title: Prompt Injection Sanitization Bypass via recent_paths and evidence_paths

title: N+1 Sequential Disk Reads during Knowledge Sync Drift Checks

In larger KBs this leads to slow Planning and Recall MCP tool response times.

title: Mismatched multi-file sync of agents.meta.json and knowledge-test.index.json

Two concurrent executions can interleave (A writes meta X, B writes meta Y, B writes index Y, A writes index X), producing on-disk state where metadata does not match the cached test index.

title: Silent index drift on contended git index.lock

fix_direction: Proactively detect a stale .git/index.lock, warn/clean up, and report git index sync issues prominently in fabric doctor instead of silently degrading.

title: Inconsistent and Bloated Error/Return Contracts in Doctor APIs
description: The doctor service surfaces three report types (DoctorReport, DoctorFixReport, DoctorApplyLintReport) with slightly different fields representing the same concept using disparate keys (fixable_errors vs fixed vs mutations; manual_errors vs remaining_manual_errors), and all duplicate the full raw report: DoctorReport inside themselves — API bloat and high integration complexity.

title: Unhandled git rebase error crashes sync continue/abort flow
description: runContinueSync and runAbortSync invoke defaultRebaseContinue / defaultRebaseAbort without catching thrown errors. title: Non-atomic session writes and unhandled parse error crash the CLI
description: saveSession writes sync-session.json with writeFileSync directly (no write-tmp+rename, no fsync); interruption/power loss corrupts it. loadSession parses via JSON.parse(readFileSync(...)) with no try-catch, so any later command (fabric sync --continue) crashes with an unhandled SyntaxError, permanently breaking sync until the session file is manually deleted.
fix_direction: Use atomicWriteJson (with fsync) in saveSession, and wrap JSON.parse in loadSession in try-catch to handle corruption gracefully (warn + return null / reclaim new session).

title: TOCTOU race and lock theft in HTTP server serve-lock
description: acquireLock verifies lock existence with fs.existsSync(p) then fs.writeFileSync(p,...), a TOCTOU race. Two concurrently starting HTTP servers can both find it empty/stale and write, one silently overwriting the other -> double execution. On shutdown releaseLock checks state.pid===process.pid and unlinks; if a new process overtook the lock, the old process's release deletes the active lock, causing accidental release.
fix_direction: Acquire atomically with fs.writeFileSync(p, content, { flag: 'wx' }) / openSync(p,'wx') so only one process succeeds; throw ServeLockHeldError if it already exists.

context.notes: Discovered by reliability perspective in DBP-20260531-103639 (engine=agy, round 2, new-ground steering; dedup deferred per user)

title: O(N^2) array linear scanning in recall ID path-filtering & intersection
description: Combined recall filters effective IDs against candidate IDs via effectiveIds.filter(id => candidateIds.includes(id)), then dedupes preserving order with a loop calling requestedIds.includes(id). Both arrays can grow to the retrieval limit N (or larger if a client passes a huge ID array), so .includes() inside .filter()/loop is O(N^2), costly at hundreds of IDs.
fix_direction: Convert candidateIds and requestedIds to Sets for O(1) .has() lookups, reducing to O(N).

title: Unbounded HTTP session growth (no cleanup / idle expiration)
description: The experimental HTTP server holds all active SSE sessions in a module-level Map. onsessionclosed only deletes on clean SSE teardown; half-open connections, network drops, or repeated init requests accumulate sessions indefinitely. With no reaping/TTL/idle timeout the session map grows unbounded, a slow memory leak and eventual OOM under high concurrent traffic.
fix_direction: Add session eviction: a background reaper for sessions idle > TTL (e.g. 5 min), or cap max concurrent sessions.

title: Redundant BM25 IDF log calculations per scored document

fix_direction: Pre-compute/cache IDF per term when building the model, or memoize idf inside scoreDoc.

title: Expensive synchronous recursive walk of all global stores in doctor checks

fix_direction: Make the walk asynchronous or cache scan results to avoid repeated synchronous I/O.

title: Explicit retrieval IDs silently truncated by semantic top-k budget
description: In combined recall, an explicit ids list is intersected with planContext semantic candidates via effectiveIds.filter(id => candidateIds.includes(id)). Because planContext truncates candidates to top_k (~30-50), any explicitly requested ID ranking poorly semantically is excluded silently, and the caller gets an empty result with no error/diagnostic that their requested rules were truncated.
fix_direction: Let explicitly requested ids bypass planContext top-k truncation, or make planContext aware of explicit ids and prioritize them so they are never omitted by the cap.

title: Non-atomic store scaffolding and race condition in initStore
description: initStore checks existsSync(identityFile) then mkdirSync + writeFileSync(identityFile,...) — not atomic. Interruption/disk-full mid-way leaves a half-initialized corrupted directory. With no lock-based concurrency control, concurrent storeCreate processes can both pass the existence check and overwrite store.json simultaneously.
fix_direction: Wrap initialization in withFileLock (or a local lock file) for mutual exclusion and roll back created folders on write errors to avoid partial-write corruption.

title: fabric store switch-write lacks mounting validation, allows invalid targets

fix_direction: Verify the alias is mounted in global config (storeList()/storeExplain()) before writing it to project config; otherwise warn or throw a descriptive error.

title: Hardcoded candidate/sample limits silently truncate forensic analysis
description: The forensic scanner hardcodes SAMPLE_LIMIT=5 and CANDIDATE_FILE_LIMIT=12, silently truncating analysis. In large projects with many entry points or multi-framework topologies, only the first 5 entry points are analyzed and at most 12 candidate files output, causing incomplete framework recommendations and missed files at scale.

title: Silent SSE ring-buffer truncation causes replay out-of-sync
description: The SSE events handler uses a fixed RING_BUFFER_CAPACITY=50 for replay history. If a client misses more than 50 events (disconnect or a burst like bulk save/re-index), older events are silently evicted; on reconnect with lastEventId the buffer cannot replay the oldest missed events, leaving the client out-of-sync with no warning.
fix_direction: Make ring-buffer capacity configurable/dynamic, or detect a lastEventId already evicted and return an explicit out_of_sync status prompting a full reload.
